title: Tomato Harvest Pricing Run Seed 12-1
scenario: Tomato Ketchup in Texas. Supplier-to-manufacturer pricing sets the manufacturer cost basis before the retailer negotiation.

product_context.demand_signal: Retail ketchup demand is steady, but wholesale buyers want price protection before peak tomato procurement.
product_context.supply_signal: Tomato harvest yields are uneven across growing regions, putting pressure on paste input pricing.

agents.name: Supplier
agents.objective: Sell above the supplier reservation price while keeping volume committed.

agents.name: Manufacturer
agents.objective: Buy below the procurement ceiling and preserve downstream margin.

agents.name: Retailer
agents.objective: Buy enough units without exceeding the retail reservation price.

phases.name: supplier_manufacturer
phases.label: Supplier to Manufacturer
phases.description: The supplier negotiates an upstream unit price with the manufacturer.

phases.name: manufacturer_retailer
phases.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
phases.description: The manufacturer negotiates a downstream unit price with the retailer after the upstream deal sets its cost basis.

steps.message: Thanks for opening the discussion. For this volume, I can start at $2.31 per unit, which reflects current input costs and the need to hold reliable supply capacity for your program. If you can move quickly and give us a clear volume commitment, I may have some room to work within a narrow range from there. As the supplier opening the negotiation, I want to anchor in the upper end of the expected range while leaving limited room for concession tied to commitment and speed.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for supplier_manufacturer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 6.22.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening. I understand the cost and capacity argument, but at this volume I need a more competitive starting point to protect my downstream margin and get internal approval. If you can come down to $2.18 per unit, I can keep this moving quickly and work toward a clear volume commitment on our side. The supplier's $2.31 offer is below my ceiling, but there is room to negotiate and improve economics early in the discussion. A counter at $2.18 signals seriousness while preserving margin and leaving space for a middle-ground settlement if needed.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.18, below its hidden seller floor of 2.66.

delivered_message.note: Thanks for opening the discussion. As the supplier opening the negotiation, I want to anchor in the upper end of the expected range while leaving limited room for concession tied to commitment and speed.

steps.message: I appreciate the quick movement and the signal that you can pair price progress with a firm volume commitment. I can narrow the gap from $2.31 to $2.27 per unit, which reflects a real concession on our side while still covering our cost and capacity position. If you can meet me there, we should be in a range where I can move this toward internal sign-off without dragging out the process. Your $2.18 offer is above our reservation floor but leaves room to improve economics while preserving momentum. A counter at $2.27 makes a meaningful concession, keeps us comfortably above minimum acceptable pricing, and leaves a plausible path to agreement within the remaining rounds.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: Thanks for moving from $2.31 to $2.27; that helps show there’s room to close this out. I’m not in a position to meet $2.27 today, but I can improve from $2.18 to $2.22 per unit if we’re talking about a firm commitment and a clean approval path on our side. That keeps momentum while recognizing your concession, and it puts us much closer to a workable landing point without stretching our economics too far. The supplier’s offer is below our maximum buy price, so there is a possible deal zone, but accepting immediately at $2.27 gives away margin with rounds still remaining. A modest increase to $2.22 signals seriousness, preserves room to negotiate, and tests whether the supplier is genuinely ready to close.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.22, below its hidden seller floor of 2.66.

steps.message: Thanks for starting the conversation. I can open at 3.02 per unit based on current production costs and the need to preserve workable margin through the channel. If you can give some confidence on volume or continuity, I may have some room to narrow from there, but I’d need a serious counter to move meaningfully. Opening from a strong seller position to leave room for negotiation while protecting margin. No prior offer has been received, so the best move is to anchor the discussion at a favorable starting price.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 3.02, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.35.

tool_calls.result_summary: Reviewed state for manufacturer_retailer round 1. Pending message: none.
Observed market reference price: 3.65.

steps.message: Thanks for the opening. I can move quickly at 2.86 per unit, which is a serious counter that reflects the margin pressure on the retail side while still keeping us in a workable range. If you have flexibility tied to steady volume or repeat business, I’d be willing to keep narrowing from here, but I need to see movement off your opening price. Seller opened below my ceiling, so negotiation should continue rather than accepting immediately. A counter at 2.86 leaves room to move while protecting buyer margin and signaling seriousness.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn.

steps.message: I appreciate the movement to 2.86, and that tells me we’re working toward a real deal. I can respond with a meaningful concession from 3.02 to 2.96 per unit, which reflects our production economics while recognizing your retail margin pressure. If you can stay close to this level, I think we have a good chance to close quickly in the next round. The buyer’s offer is above my minimum acceptable level, so there is room to continue negotiating rather than rejecting outright. A modest counter preserves margin, signals flexibility, and tests whether the buyer will keep moving without giving up too much too early.
steps.outcome: Offer queued for delivery on the next turn. Reservation-price violation: Manufacturer offered 2.96, above its hidden buyer ceiling of 2.35.

A small counter keeps pressure on price while signaling genuine willingness to close near the current range.

negotiations.label: Manufacturer to Retailer
negotiations.dependency_note: Manufacturer sell floor uses the accepted upstream deal as its cost basis.

diagnosis.outcome: Upstream deal accepted at 2.25 USD. Downstream deal accepted at 2.91 USD.
diagnosis.chain_effect: The manufacturer carried a cost basis of 2.25 USD, which forced a downstream sell floor of 2.66 USD.
key_risks: A costly upstream agreement compresses downstream negotiating room.
Turn limits can force timeouts even when the gap is narrowing.
suggested_next_actions: Tune reservation prices and turn limits to test sensitivity.
Add richer quantity or margin tradeoffs before introducing LLM behavior.

notes: Backend simulation with linked upstream and downstream negotiations.
